content.assessment: From a school network planning perspective, the status-quo counterfactual leaves geographic access, capacity, and transport outcomes to be determined by ad hoc administrative discretion and uneven local social capital rather than by network-level criteria. Given the strong demographic pressure of a shrinking school-age cohort and a rural-to-town population shift, informal negotiation offers no mechanism to plan capacity or transport provision proactively, and the historical precedent of top-down consolidation waves suggests the current framework will not prevent similarly disruptive, non-rule-based closures. Network resilience is weak because survival depends on discretionary intervention rather than systematic assessment of access or capacity thresholds.
opportunities: A future statutory framework could formalize the currently informal survival logic into transparent geographic-access criteria
strengths: Requires no new statutory infrastructure, so it does not disrupt whatever access arrangements currently exist
Allows flexible, locally responsive outcomes in cases where informal political-administrative intervention favors retention
threats: Continued population decline will keep shrinking enrollment pools feeding rural schools, increasing pressure for ad hoc closures without network-level mitigation (F-live-school_network_planning-01)
Declining rural in-migration removes a countervailing demographic force that had previously supported village school viability (F-live-school_network_planning-02)
Inter-municipal competition for pupils, documented in Hungarian micro-regions, can distort which schools survive independent of genuine geographic-efficiency logic (F-live-school_network_planning-09)
weaknesses: No rule-based procedure exists for demographic decline, so closure/access decisions occur without systematic geographic or capacity assessment (F-live-school_network_planning-04)
Reliance on informal mobilization produces geographically inconsistent access outcomes among villages with comparable enrollment profiles (F-live-school_network_planning-05)

Historical precedent shows large-scale network contraction can occur independent of demographic demand alone, indicating the current governance form provides no protective planning function (F-live-school_network_planning-03)
